text:
Admission Date:  2019-12-05              Discharge Date:   1996-09-01

Date of Birth:  1981-05-26             Sex:   F

Service: SURGERY

Allergies:
Oxycodone / Hydrocodone

Attending:Megan
Chief Complaint:
Abdominal pain, N/V/D

Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
1989-12-23: PICC placement
1984-12-23: CT-guided abdominal fluid aspiration
2003-02-09: Wide external drainage of infected pancreatic
pseudocyst and ascites, and placement of a feeding GJ tube
1980-08-19: Technically successful ultrasound-guided placement of
an 8 French drainage catheter into a left
abdominal/peripancreatic collection
2013-01-14: Exploratory laparotomy with adhesiolysis, washout wide
external drainage, repair of colotomy, and creation of loop
ileostomy.
1994-07-03: Two CT-guided drainage catheters placed with 10 French
pigtails in right lateral and left lateral abdominopelvic
collections.
1974-05-14: Technically successful fluoroscopically-guided
bilateral drainage catheter exchange and upsize to 14 French
drains to a left and right flank fluid collections.
1972-12-26: Successful CT guided upsizing of bilateral
peripancreatic fluid collection drainage catheters.
1983-01-12: Removal of drain under anesthesia.
1997-02-03: Successful ultrasound-guided pelvic fluid collection
drain
placement via a transvaginal approach.




History of Present Illness:
34 y/o female with history of triglyceride-induced pancreatitis
requiring Hendry Regional Medical Center admission in 12/2014, admitted from
2006-01-20 with N/V/D secondary to presumed
gastroenteritis, who re-presents with continued N/V/D and
intermittent crampy abdominal pain. Most recently, pt was dc-ed
from hospital and was at home from the hospital for 3 days when
she developed worsening, spasmotic abd pain today accompanied by
nausea and bilious emesis x1. She describes pain as episodic and
spasmotic. She also endorses diarrhea, but also reports having
had longstanding diarrhea 08-25 lactose intolerance. Nonbloody,
watery diarrhea has been worse over the past 3 weeks and has
been unchanged. She reports that within tha past 3 weeks her
diarrhea.
Pt developed the presumed gastroenteritis 3 weeks ago, with
N/V/abd pain, and diarrhea. Pt reports current symptoms feel
more like continuation of gastroenteritis than like her
pancreatitis. When she had pancreatitits, pain was epigastric
and radiating to the back.
In the ED, initial VS were 98.4, 55, 131/89, 16, 98% RA. On
exam, appears dry, benign abdominal exam. Labs notable for
normal WBC, normal LFTs, lipase, and lactate. No imaging done.
Patient was given zofran and 1mg IV dilaudid. VS prior to
transfer were 98.4, 61, 127/82, 16, 99% RA.
.
On arrival to the floor, patient reports feeling intermittent
pain and nausea. She had 1 episode of emesis on the floor.
.
REVIEW OF SYSTEMS: She denies F/chills, dysuria, SOB, chest
pain, recent ETOH, had 2 episodes of diarrhea today.

Past Medical History:
- Depression
- Obesity
- Hypertriglyceridemia
- Severe pancreatitis
- Anemia
- Pulmonary embolism (in 02 PE, no known DVT)
- S/p c-Section x2
- lactose intolerance


Social History:
Lives with her boyfriend and his children. She reports that her
boyfriend has been supportive. She has not been drinking EtOH
recently and denies smoking or illicit drugs.


Family History:
No history of pancreatitis or hypertriglyceridemia.
sister- healthy, [**Name2 (NI) **]- healthy

Physical Exam:
ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAM:
VS T 98.1 BP 156/112 HR 84 RR 16 98RA
GEN Alert, oriented, uncomfotable from epigastric pain
HEENT NCAT MMM EOMI sclera anicteric, OP clear
NECK supple, no JVD, no LAD, well-healed scar from trach
PULM Good aeration, CTAB no wheezes, rales, rhonchi
CV RRR normal S1/S2, no mrg
ABD soft NT ND normoactive bowel sounds, no r/g
EXT WWP 2+ pulses palpable bilaterally, no c/c/e
NEURO CNs2-12 intact, motor function grossly normal
SKIN no ulcers or lesions

1986-02-13 CT ABD:
IMPRESSION:
1. No significant interval change in size of large fluid
collection
surrounding the pancreatic head.  Perhaps minimal interval
increase in size of left abdominal fluid collection surrounding
the pancreatic tail.  Two small loculated fluid collections in
the left pelvis as above.
2. No evidence of pancreatic necrosis or infection of the above
collections at this time.
3. Interval slight worsening of left basilar partial atelectasis
and small
left pleural effusion.

1973-11-06 CT ABD:
IMPRESSION:
1. Findings again consistent with sequelae of pancreatitis. No
pancreatic
necrosis. Large communicating peritoneal fluid collection most
consistent with a chronic non-necrotic fluid collection related
to pancreatitis, is
essentially stable without specific imaging evidence to suggest
infection.
2. No evidence of abdominal/retroperitoneal hemorrhage as
questioned.
3. Increased amount of free fluid in the right hemi-abdomen
without new
discrete collection formation.
4. Narrowed splenic vein due to mass effect by adjacent fluid
collection and pancreatic inflammatory change, without
occlusion. No arterial complication of pancreatitis noted.
5. Increased left and new right pleural effusions.

2014-02-20 MRCP:
IMPRESSION:
1. Complex walled-off extra-pancreatic fat necrosis, similar in
size to
1973-11-06.  Splenic vein is severely attenuated and only
partially
visualized.  SMV is severely attenuated.
2. Increased free intraperitoneal fluid, now small-to-moderate
in amount.
3. New dilatation of small and large bowel, incompletely imaged.
 If there is clinical concern for bowel obstruction, this may be
further evaluated with CT.
4. Minimally edematous pancreas without evidence of necrosis.
Normal
pancreatic duct and bile ducts.
5. Bilateral pleural effusions, left greater than right, similar
to prior,
with adjacent compressive atelectasis.

1975-10-31 CT ABD:
IMPRESSION:
1. Large communicating peritoneal fluid collection with
peripheral
enhancement slightly decreased since the prior study.
2. Two left-sided abdominal drains, one with tip lying anterior
to fluid
collection.  Two right-sided abdominal drains, one coursing
along liver
capsule and not terminating within a fluid collection.
3. New dilation of multiple loops of fluid filled distal small
bowel and
colon to the level of the splenic flexure and left sided fluid
collection. Findings could be secondary to inflammation and
ileus at this level with
partial bowel obstruction not entirely excluded.
4. Proximal splenic vein not well visualized as before.
5. Slightly decreased left pleural effusion.

1983-04-06 CT ABD:
IMPRESSION:
1. Dilated colonic and distal small bowel loops with air-fluid
levels and
fecalization localizing to the splenic flexure of the colon.
Decompressed
distal left colon is noted.  This may represent a partial
colonic obstruction. Recommend continued followup.
2. Improving but persistent bilateral pancreatic fluid
collections as
described, improved most on the left.  Pigtail catheter remains
in place in the mid portion of the left collection.  Removal of
three of the four JP drains with stable remaining left-sided JP
drain not residing within a fluid collection.

1973-04-03 CT ABD:
IMPRESSION:
1. Findings consistent with frank perforation of hollow viscera.
 Large
amount of complicated intraperitoneal ascites, free air, as well
as
peritonitis.  Dilated colon and small bowel with question small
bowel
pneumatosis.
2. Slight interval decrease in size of large peripancreatic
fluid
collections, with left flank drainage catheter unchanged.
3. Bibasilar subsegmental atelectasis and bilateral small
pleural effusions.

2010-08-13 CT ABD:
IMPRESSION:
Multiple intraperitonal and retroperitoneal fluid collections as
described
above with several smaller collections.  Overall, there has been
interval
decrease in the amount of gas and stool within the peritoneal
cavity in this patient status post exploratory laparotomy with
transverse colotomy repair and loop ileostomy. Left
retroperitoneal collection drain has been removed since 2013-01-14.


2004-06-07 CT ABD:
IMPRESSION:
1. Multiple loculated collections within the abdomen and pelvis.
New
collections in the mesentery and mild interval increase in
prerectal
collection.
2. Two IR drains and one surgical drain in position.
3. Narrowed but patent splenic vein.
4. Small bilateral pleural effusions with associated
atelectasis.

2004-06-07 CT CHEST:
IMPRESSION:
1. Multiple loculated collections within the abdomen and pelvis.
New
collections in the mesentery and mild interval increase in
prerectal
collection.
2. Two IR drains and one surgical drain in position.
3. Narrowed but patent splenic vein.
4. Small bilateral pleural effusions with associated
atelectasis.

1976-08-11 TRANSVAGINAL US:
IMPRESSION:  Persisting collection of the portions of the fluid
components and portions of more solid-appearing components
likely representing organizing hematoma/blood products.

2012-12-17 CT ABD:
IMPRESSION:
1. Multiple loculated collections within the abdomen and pelvis
with drains insitu. Slight interval decreases in size of some
collections and marginal increases in other collections, but no
significantly increased or new collections.
2. Narrowed but patent splenic vein and superior mesenteric
vein.
3. Rim hyperdensity within a collapsed small collection in the
anterior left abdomen which has decreased in size when compared
to the prior scan.  Because of its hyperdensity, extravasation
of oral contrast cannot be completely excluded although marked
peripheral enhancement of a collapsed collection is felt more
likely. A non-contrast CT could be of further benefit in
assessment






Brief Hospital Course:
Brief Hospital Summary: 34F with PMHx s/f recent pancreatitis
08-25 hypertriglyceridemia and recent hospitalization for presumed
gastroenteritis presents with 1d recent worsening of abd pain,
n/v, with diarrhea c/f acute pancreatitis vs. gastroenteritis
vs. other chronic causes. Initial admission to medical service
with subsequent transfer to the surgical HPB service under Dr.
Sherry.

Active Issues:

# Abd pain/n/v: Pt was found to have peripancreatic fluid
collections which were present prior to admission however they
had increased in size during this admission. The abdominal fluid
was sampled and 400mL removed, found to have high LDH, no cells
but lots of cellular debris, Cx MRSA positive. She was put on
broad spectrum antibiotics for the intra-abdominal infection
which was MRSA+. Despite being on vancomycin, cefepime, and
Flagyl to cover for a possible polymicrobial infection, since
the fluid collections appeared loculated, she continued to have
SIRS physiology (tachy, fever).  Pt is currently on broad
spectrum abx with MRSA coverage with vancomycin although it has
been difficult keeping pt vanco trough in therapeutic range.
Hepatobiliary surgery consulted for possible surgical drainage
of loculated fluid due to concern for polymicrobial infection.
Now being transferred to hepatobiliary surgery

# PE, LUE DVT: Pt had a PE in 02 at rehab with SOB. No known
DVT source at that time. Now has LUE DVT in the setting of
therapeutic INR (INR was 4.0 at the time of UE clot). Per Heme,
these both seem to represent provoked clots so unlikely to have
hypercoag state. She will need lovenox 1 mg/kg sc BID for 6
months duration from original PEs (6/1989). Hypercoagulability
work showed lupus AC neg, thrombin level wnl. B/l LENIs to eval
for clot on 11-09 showed no LE DVT.
-Continue lovenox for 6 months from PE in 01/1999

The patient was transferred to the Pancreatico-biliary Surgery
Service on 1970-12-11. She underwent ultrasound-guided aspiration
of a retroperitoneal collection via a left flank approach and
fluid was sent for cultures and was positive for MRSA. On
2003-02-09 patient was taken to OR secondary to persistent fevers
and emesis, where she underwent wide external drainage of
infected pancreatic pseudocyst and ascites, placement of a
feeding GJ tube and 4 drains. The patient received 2 units of
pRBC preop and 2 units of pRBC intraop for low HCT. Post op
patient was transferred in ICU s/t tachycardia. The patient's
tachycardia was managed with IV Lopressor and improved on POD #
2 (09-05). The patient was continued on IV antibiotics, TPN, and
she had an epidural catheter and Dilaudid PCA for pain control.
The patient was transferred on the floor on 09-05. On 03-30
patient was started on tubefeed and TPN was stopped. The patient
was continued on vancomycin, cefepime and flagyl, ID followed
the patient. The patient's TF was advanced to goal and she was
transitioned to PO medications on 02-03 via J-tube. Repeat CT on
07-03 demonstrated large left retro-peritoneal fluid collection.
On 12-21 patient underwent US-guided drainage and an 8 French
drain catheter placement into a left intraabdominal fluid
collection. The fluid was positive for Staph Aureus Coag +. The
patient's G-tube was continued to gravity drainage to gravity
secondary for severe nausea. On 07-17 cefepime and flagyl were
discontinued, the patient was continued on vancomycin only for
MRSA. The Lovenox SC was restarted for treatment of PE on 02-15
and her JP # 4 was removed. 01-24 JP # 2 was removed and JP # 1
was removed on 08-24. The patient continued to have severe nausea,
which required multiple antiemetic medications around the clock.
On 08-24, she vomited large amount of TF and tubefeed was held.
She continued to have emesis on 05-01 and tubefeed was held. 09-17,
abdominal CT was obtained, which showed stable intraabdominal
fluid collections and partial colonic obstruction. The patient
was started on aggressive bowel regiment and TF was stopped. On
02-13, JP # 3 was discontinued. The patient was started on TPN,
secondary to severe nausea and TF intolerance. The Vancomycin
was discontinued on 05-08 as patient finished 3 weeks course. The
TPN was advanced to goal and was cycled.

On 03-27, patient became hypotensive with SBP 70s, she was given
2.5L bolus without response. The patient was transferred in ICU
and started on pressors. CT scan demonstrated transverse colon
perforation. The patient was taken in OR emergently, she
underwent exploratory laparotomy with adhesiolysis, washout wide
external drainage, repair of colotomy, and creation of loop
ileostomy. Resection was not undertaken given involvement of
transverse mesocolon in pancreatitis and because patient
remained unstable intraoperatively. The patient was transferred
to the ICU post op s/t hypotension and tachycardia. The patient
was started on IV Vancomycin and Zosyn. Her cultures were
positive for MRSA. The TPN was discontinued and TF was restarted
on 06-11. The patient was weaned off the pressors on 06-11. On
10-24, abdominal CT was obtained and demonstrated multiple
intraabdominal and pelvic fluid collections. On 10-09 patient
underwent CT guided drainage catheters placement into left and
right intraabdominal fluid collections, simple collection around
liver was drained without drain placement. 01-09 patient was
extubated, she was started on IV Lasix s/t fluid overload and
started on Fluconazole s/t yeast in her drain. On 11-05, patient
was transfused with 2 units of pRBC as her HCT was 21.7. Post
transfusion HCT was 27.7. On 10-29 she underwent abdominal CT,
which demonstrated that two largest fluid collection with the
drain and their size decreased. On 08-21 patient continued to
receive IV Lasix in order to diureses. On 08-06, patient
underwent fluoroscopically-guided bilateral drainage catheter
exchange and upsize to 14 French drains to a left and right
flank fluid collections. The wound VAC was placed in midline
incision. The patient started in Imodium s/t large ostomy
output. On 07-14 patient started to spike fevers, and CT scan was
obtained on 05-10. CT demonstrated multiple loculated collections
within the abdomen and pelvis and new collections in the
mesentery and mild interval increase in prerectal collection.
Patient was transfused with 2 units of pRBC for HCT 21.6, her
post transfusion HCT was 27.5. The patient continued to have
fevers. which were treated with Tylenol. On 01-17 patient
underwent CT-guided up sizing of bilateral peripancreatic fluid
collection drainage catheters. Catheters were placed to wall
suction after procedure. Vancomycin, Zosyn and Fluconazole per
ID, her WBC remained elevated. Patient's platelets continued to
rise, she was started on Aspirin. On 08-12, she went in OR and
her Malecot drain was removed under general anaesthesia. On 06-20
patient underwent successful ultrasound-guided pelvic fluid
collection drain placement via a transvaginal approach. The
patient's fluid collection grew Enterococcus sensitive to
Vancomycin. 08-24 pelvic US was obtain, which showed stable
pelvic collection, drain was left in. 07-21, Fluconazole was
discontinued and IV Micafingin was started  per ID recs. The
patient became afebrile after Micafingin, WBC still elevated.
02-13, abdominal CT scan was obtained, CT demonstrated multiple
loculated collections within the abdomen and pelvis with drains
in situ, slight interval decreases in size of some collections
and marginal
increases in other collections, but no significantly increased
or new collections. The patient's right flank drain was put to
gravity, Left drain remained on wall suction. Transvaginal drain
was removed and Foley was discontinued. On 09-26 patient was
discharged in Rehab in stable condition.
Review by the systems:
Neuro: The patient currently receiving Dilaudid via J-tube, pain
is well controlled.
CV: Was tachycardic and hypotensive post op required ICU
admission, pressors support and IV Metoprolol. Currently on PO
Metoprolol, continue to have intermittent tachycardia required
IV Lopressor prn.
Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary
standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary
toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirrometry were
encouraged throughout hospitalization.
GI: The patient currently continued on TF up to goals. She
tolerates clear liquid diet as well. The ostomy output remains
high, but improving with PO Imodium.
ID: The patient's white blood count remains elevated, she is
afebrile. Continued on IV Vanc/Zosyn/Micafungin , ID will
follow.  Wound VAC in place, please change dressing Q72H.
Endocrine: No issues
Hematology: Anemia of chronic disease, required two transfusion
during hospitalization, Currently stable with HCT 28-29.
Prophylactics: Had history of PE, currently on Lovenox SC BID.









Medications on Admission:
Preadmission medications listed are correct and complete.
Information was obtained from Patient.
1. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg PO BID
hold for sbp<100 and hr <60
2. Albuterol Inhaler 10-14 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN
SOB/bronchospasm/wheezing
3. BuPROPion (Sustained Release) 150 mg PO BID
4. traZODONE 100 mg PO HS
5. Tricor *NF* (fenofibrate nanocrystallized) 145 mg Oral daily
6. Warfarin 1.5 mg PO DAILY16


Discharge Medications:
1. BuPROPion 100 mg PO TID
ok to crush
2. Metoprolol Tartrate 50 mg PO QID
Hold for BP< 90, HR<60
3. traZODONE 100 mg PO HS:PRN insomnia
4. Tricor *NF* (fenofibrate nanocrystallized) 145 mg Oral daily
hypertriglyceridemia Reason for Ordering: Wish to maintain
preadmission medication while hospitalized, as there is no
acceptable substitute drug product available on formulary.
patient has recent severe necrotizing pancreatitis secondary to
hypertriglicerydemia and must be placed back on her Tricor.
5. Acetaminophen 892-924-2660 mg PO Q6H pain or fever
6. Aspirin 325 mg PO DAILY
Please hold AM dose on 1997-02-03
7. Heparin Flush (10 units/ml) 2 mL IV PRN line flush
PICC, heparin dependent: Flush with 10mL Normal Saline followed
by Heparin as above daily and PRN per lumen.
8. HYDROmorphone (Dilaudid) 2-4 mg PO Q3H:PRN pain
9. Loperamide 2 mg PO BID
10. Micafungin 100 mg IV Q24H fungal infection
11. Miconazole Powder 2% 1 Appl TP Bhc Mesilla Valley Hospital:PRN itching
12. Ondansetron 8 mg IV Q6H:PRN nausea
13. Piperacillin-Tazobactam 4.5 g IV Q8H
14. Promethazine 12.5 mg IV Q6H:PRN nausea
15. Sarna Lotion 1 Appl TP Bhc Mesilla Valley Hospital:PRN itching
16. Vancomycin 1000 mg IV Q 12H
Please send Vanco trough before 4th dose
17. Albuterol Inhaler 10-14 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN
SOB/bronchospasm/wheezing
18. Enoxaparin Sodium 90 mg SC Q12H


Discharge Disposition:
Extended Care

Facility:
James H. Quillen Va Medical Center - 7780 Marissa Route Apt. 778
Waterston, NC 73623

Discharge Diagnosis:
MRSA intra-abdominal infection
Pancreatitis
Pancreatic insufficiency
Upper extremity DVT
Anemia
Electrolyte abnormalities


Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.


Discharge Instructions:
You were admitted to the surgery service at Choctaw Memorial Hospital for treatment
of an acute pancreatitis and pancreatic pseudocyst. Your
recovery was complicated by colon perforation, which required
surgical intervention; multiple infected intraabdominal
abcsesses, which required prolong antibiotics treatment. Now
your condition is stable to be discharged in Rehab to continue
your recovery with following instructions:
Please resume all regular home medications , unless specifically
advised not to take a particular medication.  Also, please take
any new medications as prescribed.
Please get plenty of rest, continue to ambulate several times
per day, and drink adequate amounts of fluids.  Avoid lifting
weights greater than 02-12 lbs until you follow-up with your
surgeon, who will instruct you further regarding activity
restrictions.
Avoid driving or operating heavy machinery while taking pain
medications.
Please follow-up with your surgeon and Primary Care Provider
(PCP) as advised.
Incision Care:
*Wound VAC would be changed by RNs every 72 hours. Please
monitor for signs and symptoms of infection. Please monitor
amount and quality of outputs.
.
IR Drain x 2:
*Keep Left drain to low wall suction. Flash and aspirate with 10
cc of NS Q8H.
*Right drain to bulb suction. Do not flash and aspirate.
*Please look at the site every day for signs of infection.
*Please note color, consistency, and amount of fluid in the
drain.  Call the doctor, nurse practitioner, or [**Month/Year (2) 269**] nurse if the
amount increases significantly or changes in character. Record
the output, if instructed to do so.
*Wash the area gently with warm, soapy water or 1/2 strength
hydrogen peroxide followed by saline rinse, pat dry, and place a
drain sponge. Change daily and as needed.
*Keep the insertion site clean and dry otherwise.
*Avoid swimming, baths, hot tubs; do not submerge yourself in
water.
*Make sure to keep the drain attached securely to your body to
prevent pulling or dislocation.
.
PICC Line:
*Please monitor the site regularly, and Samuel Green your MD, nurse
practitioner, or Alexandria Smith Nurse if you notice redness, swelling,
tenderness or pain, drainage or bleeding at the insertion site.
* Donna Davidson your MD Sheri Baker to the Emergency Room immediately if
the PICC Line tubing becomes damaged or punctured, or if the
line is pulled out partially or completely.  DO NOT USE THE PICC
LINE IN THESE CIRCUMSTANCES.Please keep the dressing clean and
dry. Contact your [**Name2 (NI) 269**] Nurse if the dressing comes undone or is
significantly soiled for further instructions.
.
GJ tube: Keep G-tube capped, may uncapped if experiencing
nausea. Please monitor for signs and symptoms of infection,
dislocation. Flush with 30 cc of tap water Q6H.
.
Monitoring Ostomy output/Prevention of Dehydration:
*Keep well hydrated.
*Replace fluid loss from ostomy daily.
*Avoid only drinking plain water. Include Gatorade and/or other
vitamin drinks to replace fluid.
*Try to maintain ostomy output between 1000mL to 1500mL per day.

*If Ostomy output >1 liter, take 4mg of Imodium, repeat 2mg with
each episode of loose stool. Do not exceed 16mg/24 hours.
